items.title: Knife used in Canberra Hospital incident 'secreted' from ACT police in earlier search

children.content: The ACT's chief police officer says the knife produced by a Canberra Hospital patient that led to an hours-long security incident had not been detected by police during a search of the man earlier in the day.

items.title: Illicit cigarettes, vapes seized in Canberra retailer raids

image.altText: Boxes of illicit cigarettes stacked in a container.

items.title: Secure area with one exit left hospital staff trapped by armed patient

children.content: No-one was injured during the incident at Canberra Hospital but staff were stuck inside a "secure staff station" for three hours while police disarmed a man with a knife.

image.altText: A sign that says "Emergency Building 5'' and "Main entry'' pointing to a building.

items.title: Hundreds of jobs to go at Home Affairs amid 'financial pressures'

children.content: The secretary of the second-largest department says she has been trying to reduce headcount by natural attrition, but that further job cuts are needed by the end of the financial year to fit within the department's budget.

image.altText: An image of the backs of two Border Force officers, their organisation clearly shown by signs on their backs

items.title: How beer and AI are changing the course of Canberra's economic future

children.content: The capital has long been known as a public service town, but with about two in three workers now employed in the private sector and the population expected to reach nearly 700,000 by 2050, is that reputation — and key economic driver — likely to change?

image.altText: A man stands and smiles, leaning on a bench in a large workshop space with buckets and gear behind him.

items.title: 'Self-belief, respect': Indigenous boxers building strength with David Pocock

items.title: The 'double-headed monster' set to push up prices at the check-out

children.content: Companies that use plastic packaging are facing price pressures and supply disruptions, which they warn will likely mean higher costs for consumers. The cost of food-grade resin out of Singapore, which is used in milk bottles, has increased 110 per cent since the start of the Middle East war.
